title: Thermometry for a Kagome Lattice Dipolar Rydberg Simulator
authors: Erik Fitzner, Igor Lesanovsky, Björn Sbierski
abstract: We propose an accurate thermometry approach for Rydberg atom tweezer arrays combining data from correlation and local susceptibility measurements with a theoretical high-temperature expansion method for dynamic spin correlations. We apply our approach to a recent quantum simulation experiment [Bornet et al., arXiv 2602.14323] realizing an anti-ferromagnetic dipolar spin-1/2 XY model on the Kagome lattice. We obtain T=0.55J and S/N=0.67 ln2 for temperature and entropy respectively, showing that further experimental efforts are required to reach the putative quantum spin liquid regime.
summary: This paper proposes a new method for measuring temperature and entropy in Rydberg atom quantum simulators. By combining experimental spin correlations with a high-temperature expansion theory, the authors analyze a Kagome lattice experiment. They find that the current experimental temperature is significantly higher than what is needed to observe quantum spin liquid physics. This work provides a way to quantify how close experimental platforms are to highly entangled quantum phases.
main_problem: Developing an accurate thermometry protocol to determine temperature and entropy in Rydberg atom tweezer arrays, specifically for frustrated Kagome lattice systems where traditional numerical methods like QMC suffer from the sign problem.
main_result: The study estimates a temperature of T=0.55J and entropy of S/N=0.67 ln2, concluding that current experimental temperatures are too high to reach the predicted quantum spin liquid regime.
method: A combination of experimental measurements of correlations and local susceptibility with a theoretical Dynamic High-Temperature Expansion (Dyn-HTE) up to 8th order.
model_or_system: An anti-ferromagnetic dipolar spin-1/2 XY model realized on a 2D Kagome lattice of 114 Rubidium-87 atoms in Rydberg tweezer arrays.
key_observables: Nearest-neighbor equal-time zz correlator (Czz1), local zz spin susceptibility (zz0), and distance-resolved static zz susceptibility (zzd).
important_parameters: Coupling strength J approx 2pi x 0.77 MHz, nearest-neighbor distance a = 12 micrometers, and interaction decay 1/r^3.
main_assumptions: The system is in a thermal state; the interaction is truncated to a 4th-nearest neighbor model for theoretical calculations; use of Padé approximants for series convergence.
figures_summary: Fig 2 shows experimental correlators and susceptibilities compared to theoretical predictions; Fig 3 displays entropy per spin as a function of temperature; Fig 4 shows convergence tests for interaction truncation; Fig 5 illustrates the diagrammatic expansion graphs.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the thermometry challenge, describes the Kagome Rydberg platform and XY model, details the Dyn-HTE method and expansion orders, presents temperature and entropy results, and concludes with implications for reaching the QSL regime.
why_interesting: It provides a critical diagnostic tool (thermometry) for Rydberg simulators and addresses the fundamental difficulty of characterizing thermalization and entropy in frustrated quantum many-body systems.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

FIG. 2. Top: Nearest-neighbor equal-time zz-correlator Czz 1 as a function of J/T. By comparison with the experimen- tal value from panel Fig. 1(b), shown as a horizontal dashed line, a corresponding temperature of J/T = 1.85(2) can be estimated. Gray regions indicate error bounds. Bottom: Distance resolved static zz-susceptibilities χzz d as a function of J/T. The experimental linear response susceptibilities χzz d from Fig. 1(c) (horizontal dashed lines) correspond to J/T = 1.81(4) and J/T = 1.7(4), respectively.
